metadata.analysis_scope: HTTP request-response pipeline, ORM integration, middleware architecture

module_map.responsibility: HTTP protocol layer: request/response objects, headers, cookies, status codes
URL routing and pattern matching: resolve paths to views, reverse URL patterns to paths
View dispatch layer: class-based views (CBV), generic views, view-to-response mapping
WSGI/ASGI entry point: request lifecycle management, middleware chain execution, exception handling
Request/response hooks: security, caching, compression, CSRF, authentication
ORM database abstraction: models, querysets, field types, migrations, connections
Template rendering engine: template loading, context rendering, filters, tags
High-level API functions: render(), redirect(), get_object_or_404(), get_list_or_404()

pipeline_trace.description: WSGI server invokes WSGIHandler.__call__(environ, start_response). Parses environ dict to extract HTTP method, path, headers, body stream.
WSGIRequest extends HttpRequest, decoding environ to populate request properties (GET, POST, COOKIES, FILES, method, path, META).
Each middleware's process_request(request) is called in order. Middleware can modify request, raise exceptions, or short-circuit by returning a response.
URLResolver.resolve(path) matches request.path_info against URLPattern regex/route patterns. Returns ResolverMatch with view callable, args, kwargs, url_name.
For class-based views (CBV), View.as_view() factory creates view callable. Request is dispatched to handler method (get, post, etc.) via View.dispatch(request, *args, **kwargs).
key_types: method-specific handler (get, post, put, delete, patch)

pipeline_trace.description: View handler executes business logic: QuerySet.filter().get(), .create(), .update(), .delete(). Each ORM operation generates SQL and executes against database connection pool.
View returns HttpResponse or TemplateResponse. Template rendering occurs (template.render(context)) and response body is finalized.
Each middleware's process_response(request, response) is called in reverse order. Final opportunity to modify response headers, body, or cookies.
Response status, headers, and body are serialized as WSGI response format. start_response(status, headers) is called, then response content is yielded as byte strings.

cross_module_hubs.reason: Central abstraction for all HTTP semantics: every stage (handlers, middleware, views, shortcuts) depends on HttpRequest/HttpResponse. Lowest-level foundational module.
Orchestrator: entry point that orchestrates the entire request lifecycle. Loads and chains middleware, invokes URL resolution, calls views, orchestrates response flow. High outbound coupling.
Critical link between request routing and view dispatch: handlers depend on URL resolution, views depend on reverse(), shortcuts depend on URL utilities. Central routing hub.

change_proposal.feature_summary: Add a middleware that captures per-request metadata (view name, ORM query count, response time) and emits structured log entries. Must support both sync and async contexts.
change_proposal.feature_description: Middleware should track: (1) request start time, (2) view name from URLResolver.resolve(), (3) database query count via django.db.connection.queries, (4) response time. Emit as structured JSON logs or structured logging library (e.g., structlog, python-json-logger).
files_to_modify: django/http/request.py (optional: attach start_time to request object)

new_types_and_classes.name: StructuredLoggingMiddleware
methods: process_request(request) -> None: Attach start_time to request; reset query counter
process_view(request, view_func, view_args, view_kwargs) -> Optional[HttpResponse]: Capture view_name from resolver_match
process_response(request, response) -> HttpResponse: Compute elapsed time, query count; emit structured log

new_types_and_classes.name: RequestMetadata
new_types_and_classes.purpose: Dataclass or NamedTuple to hold per-request state (start_time, view_name, query_count)

change_proposal.pattern_to_follow: Existing Django middleware patterns: (1) Inherit from MiddlewareMixin (django/utils/deprecation.py), (2) Implement process_request, process_view, process_response methods, (3) Store per-request state on request object (e.g., request._logging_metadata), (4) Support both sync and async via classonlymethod view_is_async check
change_proposal.integration_point: Middleware stack in django/core/handlers/wsgi.py and django/core/handlers/asgi.py via BaseHandler.load_middleware(). Register in settings.MIDDLEWARE.

request_lifecycle_hooks: process_view: extract view_name from request.resolver_match.url_name, store on request
process_response: calculate response_time, query_count; emit structured log
implementation_details.query_counting_mechanism: django.db.connection.queries is a list of query dicts (only enabled if settings.DEBUG=True). Alternative: use django-debug-toolbar's query logging or custom SQL middleware hook.
implementation_details.async_support: Use asgiref.sync.sync_to_async() for blocking logging I/O; check for asyncio.iscoroutinefunction(view_func) in process_view
implementation_details.structured_logging_format: Emit JSON with fields: {timestamp, request_id, method, path, view_name, status_code, response_time_ms, query_count, user_id}

risks.mitigation: Make logging hooks optional via settings; use sampling for high-volume endpoints; ensure query counting doesn't trigger additional queries
Gracefully disable query counting when DEBUG=False; document this limitation; consider custom instrumentation hook in models/query.py

risks.risk: Async context complexity: logging.Logger is thread-safe but not async-safe
risks.mitigation: Use thread-safe queue-based logging (QueueHandler, QueueListener) or structlog (async-aware); avoid blocking I/O in middleware

risks.risk: Middleware ordering: if placed incorrectly, may not capture all exceptions or early-exit responses
risks.mitigation: Document recommended position (early in MIDDLEWARE list); add process_exception hook for exception cases
Make structured logging opt-in; use standard library logging as fallback; don't require external dependency

risks.risk: Privacy: logging query count or response time could expose sensitive timing information
risks.mitigation: Make all fields configurable; add user/IP filtering; document data retention policies

related_modules.django.conf.settings: Configuration for log level, sampling, query counting enable

testing_considerations: Mock time.perf_counter() to test response time calculation
Mock django.db.connection.queries to verify query count
Test with both sync and async views (use asgiref.sync.async_to_sync)
Verify middleware doesn't interfere with exception handling (process_exception)
Profile with Django Debug Toolbar to verify no regression

tool_usage.purpose: High-level overview of core package structure
Understand URL resolution pipeline (URLResolver, URLPattern, ResolverMatch classes)
Map HTTP module exports (HttpRequest, HttpResponse variants)
Trace WSGI entry point and request lifecycle (WSGIHandler, WSGIRequest)
Understand view dispatch mechanism (View.as_view, View.dispatch, handler methods)
Map ORM module exports and dependency structure
Verify BaseHandler definition and callers (none found; low coupling)
Verify middleware base class definition and usage patterns
